Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: The user wants a DuckDB SQL query that does several things First I need to construct the process variants by ordering activities per case by timestamp Then group cases by these variants count them find the top K and finally return all events from those top K variants

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Starting with the first part getting the ordered sequence of activities for each case In DuckDB I can use window functions For each row in the event_log I need to group by case_id and then order by timestamp Then I can aggregate the activities into an array So maybe using STRING_AGG with ORDER BY but wait DuckDB has ARRAY_AGG which might be better since it preserves order So for each case_id collect the activities in the order of their timestamps into an array That would give the process variant for each case

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: So the first CTE Common Table Expression would be something like WITH ordered_activities AS SELECT case_id ARRAY_AGG activity ORDER BY timestamp AS activity_sequence FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Next step is to group these sequences into variants and count how many cases are in each

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: So another CTE variants AS SELECT activity_sequence COUNT case_id AS variant_count FROM ordered_activities GROUP BY activity_sequence

Name: Abductive Reasoning - IND
Text: Then we need to get the top K variants by variant_count But how to handle ties The problem says top K variants by their frequency so if there are ties at the Kth position maybe include all of them But the problem statement might just want exactly K but the user probably expects to use RANK or DENSE_RANK

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: Lets assume that if there are ties we include all of them But the problem says top K variants by their frequency so perhaps using a LIMIT K

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: top_k_variants AS SELECT activity_sequence FROM variants ORDER BY variant_count DESC LIMIT K

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Once we have the top K variants we need to get all the cases that belong to those variants So we need to join back to the ordered_activities CTE to get the case_ids that have activity_sequence in the top K variants

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: case_ids_in_top_k AS SELECT o case_id FROM ordered_activities o INNER JOIN top_k_variants t ON o activity_sequence equals t activity_sequence

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Then finally we need to return all events from the original event_log where case_id is in case_ids_in_top_k

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Putting it all together WITH ordered_activities AS SELECT case_id ARRAY_AGG activity ORDER BY timestamp AS activity_sequence FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id variants AS SELECT activity_sequence COUNT case_id AS variant_count FROM ordered_activities GROUP BY activity_sequence top_k_variants AS SELECT activity_sequence FROM variants ORDER BY variant_count DESC LIMIT K case_ids_in_top_k AS SELECT o case_id FROM ordered_activities o INNER JOIN top_k_variants t ON o activity_sequence equals t activity_sequence SELECT all FROM event_log WHERE case_id IN SELECT case_id FROM case_ids_in_top_k

Name: Validation - IND
Text: But wait in DuckDB the LIMIT clause requires a numeric value So if K is a variable the user would need to replace K with a number

Name: Counterfactual Reasoning - IND
Text: Another thing to consider when two variants have the same count how to handle them For example if K is 3 but there are two variants with the same count as the third should they be included To handle ties we might need to use RANK or DENSE_RANK

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: However the problem statement is not clear and the initial approach using LIMIT K is acceptable unless specified otherwise So proceeding with the initial approach

Name: Validation - PE
Text: The activity_sequence is an array and comparing arrays in DuckDB is possible So when joining ordered_activities with top_k_variants on activity_sequence it should correctly match the arrays

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Testing for correctness case 1 has sequence A B case 2 has A C case 3 has A B So variants are A B count 2 and A C count 1 If K equals 1 top variant is A B so cases 1 and 3 are included The final result should include all events from cases 1 and 3 The query should correctly aggregate the sequences count them select the top K and filter the events

Name: Validation - PE
Text: The problem says sorted events within that case by their timestamp so the query uses ORDER BY timestamp which is correct as written
